ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sun-dried tomato as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sun-dried tomato is approximately 14g (1 tablespoon), used as a flavour garnish or condiment in salads, pasta, and sandwiches.

nutrient_source.title: Sun-dried tomatoes — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Tomatoes, sun-dried (NDB 11957)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F14050006, confidence 0.95). Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11957 (Tomatoes, sun-dried) for secondary nutrient completeness. HPB values used where available; USDA SR Legacy used to fill saturated fat and micronutrient gaps. Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Dried Tomatoes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for dried fruit/vegetable concentrates and peer-reviewed literature on tomato GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for sun-dried tomato was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on: (1) fresh tomato GI of approximately 15 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) concentration of sugars through dehydration raises effective GI modestly; (3) very high dietary fibre content (12.3g/100g) substantially attenuates glycaemic response; (4) peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008) supports low GI classification for high-fibre dried vegetables. GL per serving = (35 × 55.76 × 0.14) / 100 = 2.7g, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium (3427mg/100g) with low sodium (107mg/100g) — potassium-to-sodium ratio strongly favourable for blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Sun-dried tomatoes are suitable for hypertension management in small portions (10–20g as a condiment) due to their extraordinary potassium content (3427mg/100g) and low sodium (107mg/100g), consistent with DASH diet principles; however, commercial brine-packed varieties may contain sodium exceeding 400mg/100g and should be avoided — prefer oil-packed or unsalted varieties and always check the label.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High concentrated sugar (37.59g/100g) from dehydration exceeds the 20g/100g glycaemic ceiling, despite mitigating high dietary fibre (12.3g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sun-dried tomatoes are unsuitable as a main ingredient for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to concentrated sugar at 37.59g/100g — well above the 20g/100g threshold — and should be restricted to small flavour garnish portions of 5–10g per serving, paired with protein and healthy fat to further blunt any postprandial glucose response; the high fibre content (12.3g/100g) partially mitigates glycaemic impact but does not offset the sugar load at typical serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.43g/100g), high dietary fibre (12.3g/100g), high potassium (3427mg/100g), and rich lycopene content support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sun-dried tomatoes are broadly favourable for cardiovascular health when consumed in small portions (10–20g) due to their very low saturated fat (0.43g/100g), high dietary fibre (12.3g/100g) which supports LDL reduction, and exceptional potassium (3427mg/100g) which supports healthy blood pressure; the concentrated lycopene content is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk in peer-reviewed literature, though the high sugar concentration warrants portion control.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable — solanine and alkaloids may trigger joint inflammation in susceptible individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sun-dried tomatoes are a nightshade vegetable and may aggravate joint inflammation in RA patients sensitive to solanine alkaloids; individual tolerance varies significantly and patients should monitor their personal response, limiting to small garnish portions if tolerated, while noting that the lycopene and potassium content offer anti-inflammatory benefits for those who can tolerate nightshades.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sun-dried tomato (Solanum lycopersicum, dried) consists of dark red, shrivelled tomato pieces produced by dehydrating ripe tomatoes under sunlight or in low-temperature ovens, concentrating their flavour, colour, and nutrients dramatically. The dehydration process reduces water content from approximately 94g/100g in fresh tomatoes to around 14.6g/100g, resulting in an intensely tangy, sweet-savoury flavour with a chewy, leathery texture. Sun-dried tomatoes are a staple of Mediterranean and Italian cuisine, used as a condiment, pizza topping, pasta ingredient, antipasto component, and sandwich filling. Nutritionally, they are exceptionally nutrient-dense per 100g: energy 258 kcal, protein 14.11g, fat 2.97g (saturated fat 0.43g), carbohydrate 55.76g, sugar 37.59g, dietary fibre 12.3g, sodium 107mg, and potassium 3427mg — one of the highest potassium concentrations of any common food. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio makes them strongly favourable for blood pressure management per DASH diet principles. The very high sugar content (37.59g/100g) results from natural fructose concentration during dehydration and makes them unsuitable as a main ingredient for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the high fibre partially attenuates glycaemic impact. They are rich in lycopene (a carotenoid antioxidant associated with reduced cardiovascular and prostate cancer risk), vitamin C (39.2mg/100g), iron (9.09mg/100g), and magnesium (194mg/100g). As a nightshade vegetable, they may be problematic for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis sensitive to solanine. Commercially available in oil-packed, brine-packed, or dry forms — sodium content varies significantly by preparation; unsalted or oil-packed varieties are preferred for health-conscious consumption.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sun-dried tomatoes?
Are sun-dried tomatoes high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F14050006, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary nutrients cross-referenced and confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11957 (Tomatoes, sun-dried). Both sources are on the approved source list. Values are consistent between sources. GI is an analogous estimate documented with peer-reviewed basis.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.43g/100g), monounsaturated fat (0.44g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (1.15g/100g), and micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11957 (Tomatoes, sun-dried) to supplement HPB primary data. 2. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for sun-dried tomatoes (plant-based food with no industrial hydrogenation). 3. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: naturally zero — sun-dried tomato is a plant-based food and contains no vitamin B12. 4. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy; plant-based foods do not naturally contain meaningful vitamin D. 5. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based food contains no cholesterol. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for sun-dried tomato was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 7. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as sun-dried tomatoes are not a grain product. 8. Commercial brine-packed sun-dried tomatoes may have sodium exceeding 400mg/100g — values here represent unsalted/plain sun-dried tomatoes as per HPB source. 9. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are defensible matches; SNOMED CT omitted as no confirmed code was identified.
